narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 55.5% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to protect the program's success rate. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your placement in the third decile for both first-year and third-year grades, which are notably lower than the top-half deciles typical among students who go on to pass the bar. Your LSAT score of 34 also played a key role, falling short of the average 37.27 seen in successful bar takers, though it was the single strongest factor pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass. In contrast, your third-year decile ranking had the next biggest push toward predicting failure, as it signaled challenges in sustaining higher performance over time compared to peers who succeeded. Your first-year decile similarly nudged the outlook downward, reinforcing concerns about early academic momentum when benchmarked against those who passed. While your middle family income aligns with over a third of bar passers and your choice to study full-time matches nearly all of them, these didn't outweigh the academic signals in the model's eyes. Overall, your profile stacks up as solid in some areas like commitment to full-time study, but the lower deciles and LSAT created a riskier picture than the stronger academic baselines of accepted applicants. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven call to prioritize bar passage rates for the school's reputation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, and focus on strategies to climb into a higher grade decile in any future undergrad or grad work, perhaps through targeted tutoring or study habits proven effective for similar students. Building a stronger academic narrative could tip future models, and many applicants succeed on reapplication after addressing these gaps. You're clearly driven, given your full-time plans, so channeling that into these improvements will make a real difference next time.
